Parameter_ID: She/her used for inanimate referents
Parameter_Description: She was burning good [about a house]

Parameter_ID: He/him used for inanimate referents
Parameter_Description: I bet thee cansnʼ climb he [= a tree]

Parameter_ID: Alternative forms/phrases for referential (non-dummy) it
Parameter_Description: e.g. the thing: When you on [‘switch on’] the alarm system you press this button. When you off the thing [‘switch it off’] you press that one.

Parameter_ID: Alternative forms/phrases for dummy it
Parameter_Description: Thass rainen ‘Itʼs raining’ (emphatic)

Parameter_ID: Generalized third person singular pronoun: subject pronouns
Parameter_Description: em for ‘he/she/it’

Parameter_ID: Generalized third person singular pronoun: object pronouns
Parameter_Description: om for ‘him/her/it’

Parameter_ID: Me instead of I in coordinate subjects
Parameter_Description: Me and my brother/My brother and me were late

Parameter_ID: Myself/meself instead of I in coordinate subjects
Parameter_Description: My/me husband and myself were late.

Parameter_ID: Benefactive “personal dative” construction
Parameter_Description: I got me a new car; She got her a new car; They got them a new car

Parameter_ID: No gender distinction in third person singular
Parameter_Description: My mother, heʼs a primary school teacher; My husband who was in England, she was by then my fiancé

Parameter_ID: Regularized reflexives paradigm
Parameter_Description: hisself, theirselves/theirself

Parameter_ID: Object pronoun forms serving as base for first and/or second person reflexives
Parameter_Description: meself for ‘myself’

Parameter_ID: Subject pronoun forms serving as base for reflexives
Parameter_Description: theyselves for ‘themselves’

Parameter_ID: No number distinction in reflexives
Parameter_Description: ourself for ‘ourselves’

Parameter_ID: Absolute use of reflexives
Parameter_Description: And by God, he said, … he’d be the devil if himself wouldn’t make him laugh; Himself is gone to Dublin (meaning ‘boss’, ‘head’)

Parameter_ID: Emphatic reflexives with own
Parameter_Description: Everybody took care of their own self.

Parameter_ID: Creation of possessive pronouns with prefix fi- +personal pronoun
Parameter_Description: Den no fi-me work me put yuh inna? [lit.: Then no for-me job I put you into] ‘Then wasnʼt it my job I got for you?’

Parameter_ID: Subject pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: first person singular
Parameter_Description: I book for ‘my book’

Parameter_ID: Subject pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: first person plural
Parameter_Description: When we done make we farm, we n’t know nothing about sell…

Parameter_ID: Subject pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: third person singular
Parameter_Description: he book for ‘his book’

Parameter_ID: Subject pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: third person plural
Parameter_Description: It’s they book

Parameter_ID: You as (modifying) possessive pronoun
Parameter_Description: Yu kyaan kom iin-ya siev yu pie yu fier ‘You canʼt come in here unless you pay your fare’

Parameter_ID: Second person pronoun forms other than you as (modifying) possessive pronoun
Parameter_Description: Shut unu ai, na! ‘Shut your [2PL] eyes, wonʼt you?’

Parameter_ID: Object pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: third person singular
Parameter_Description: im dog ‘his dog’; get im gear on

Parameter_ID: Object pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: third person plural
Parameter_Description: them book for ‘their book’

Parameter_ID: Object pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: first person singular
Parameter_Description: Heʼs me brother; Iʼve lost me bike

Parameter_ID: Object pronoun forms as (modifying) possessive pronouns: first person plural
Parameter_Description: Us George was a nice one; We like us town;

Parameter_ID: Use of us + NP in subject function
Parameter_Description: Us kids used to pinch the sweets like hell.

Parameter_ID: Use of us in object function with singular referent
Parameter_Description: Show us [‘me’] them boots.

Parameter_ID: Non-coordinated subject pronoun forms in object function
Parameter_Description: e.g. You did get he out of bed in the middle of the night; Well, if I didnʼt know they, they knowed I

Parameter_ID: Non-coordinated object pronoun forms in subject function
Parameter_Description: Evercreech, what did ʼem [ʼtheyʼ] call it?; Us say ʼerʼs [‘she is’] dry; Usʼll do it; Huz san him? ‘Whose son is he?’

Parameter_ID: Distinction between emphatic vs. non-emphatic forms of pronouns
Parameter_Description: in vs. ì: ì bì in [emphatic 3SG] we ì [unstressed 3SG] mek govanment no put mo presha fo wi. [lit.: It be that what it make government not put more pressure for we] ‘That is what prevents the government from using more pressure on us.’

Parameter_ID: Independent possessive pronoun forms with added nasal
Parameter_Description: hern, hisn, ourn; hersn, oursn, ourns

Parameter_ID: Forms or phrases for the second person plural pronoun other than you
Parameter_Description: youse; yʼall; aayʼ; yufela; you…together; all of you; you ones/ʼuns; you guys; you people

Parameter_ID: Forms or phrases for the second person singular pronoun other than you
Parameter_Description: ye

Parameter_ID: Distinct forms for inclusive/exclusive first person non-singular
Parameter_Description: afla (inclusive, i.e. ‘we, including you’) vs. mifela (exclusive, i.e. ‘we, not including you’)

Parameter_ID: More number distinctions in personal pronouns than simply singular vs. plural
Parameter_Description: hem (3SG), tufala (3DU), trifala (3TRI), ol(geta) (3PL)

Parameter_ID: Specialized plural markers for pronouns
